steps.title: Mijn Hoofd, Mijn Buik
steps.desc: Before you can talk about health, you need the body. Dutch body part vocabulary is full of Germanic cognates — head/hoofd, hand/hand, foot/voet. This lesson teaches 12 essential body parts and the construction 'Ik heb pijn in mijn...' (I have pain in my...).

steps.example: Ik heb pijn in mijn hoofd.
steps.exampleEn: I have pain in my head.
steps.note:
Het-word. Formal; informal = 'het hoofd' or just 'kop'.
'Hoofdpijn' = headache (compound word).

De-word. 'Buikpijn' = stomachache.
'Mijn buik doet pijn' = my belly hurts.
Alternative: 'de maag' (the stomach, internal organ).

De-word. 'Rugpijn' = back pain.
Very common complaint in the Netherlands.
Related to German 'Rücken'.

De-word. Identical to English!
Plural: handen. Final -d sounds like -t.

De-word. Plural: voeten.
Same root as English 'foot'. The 'oe' = 'oo'.

steps.example: Ik heb pijn in mijn been.
steps.exampleEn: I have pain in my leg.
steps.note:
Het-word. ⚠️ False friend in English!
Dutch 'been' = leg (AND bone).
English 'bean' = Dutch 'boon'. Different word!

De-word. Identical to English and German.
Plural: armen.

steps.example: Ik heb pijn in mijn oor.
steps.exampleEn: I have pain in my ear.
steps.note:
Het-word. Plural: oren.
Same root as English 'ear' and German 'Ohr'.

Het-word. Plural: ogen.
The 'g' in 'oog' is the Dutch throat sound.

De-word. 'Keelpijn' = sore throat.
Very common winter complaint.

steps.title: 🏥 Compound Pain Words
steps.text:
Dutch builds pain words by compounding:

hoofdpijn = headache (hoofd + pijn)
buikpijn = stomachache
rugpijn = back pain
keelpijn = sore throat
kiespijn = toothache (kies = molar)
oorpijn = earache

The pattern: [body part] + pijn = [body part]-ache.

Alternatively: 'Ik heb pijn in mijn [body part].'
Ik heb pijn in mijn been. = My leg hurts.
Ik heb pijn in mijn arm. = My arm hurts.

Or: '[body part] doet pijn.' = [body part] hurts.
Mijn buik doet pijn. = My stomach hurts.
Mijn hoofd doet pijn. = My head hurts.

steps.exampleEn: I have a lot of pain.
steps.note:
De-word. 'Pijn hebben' = to have pain.
'Pijn doen' = to hurt.
Both constructions are equally common.

steps.hint: I have PAIN in my back
My belly hurts. I have a stomachache.
The paired/sensory ones tend to be het-words

steps.title: Ik Voel Me...
steps.desc: Dutch uses reflexive verbs for feelings — you learned the system in Unit 5. Now apply it to emotions and wellbeing. 'Ik voel me goed', 'ik voel me ziek', 'ik voel me moe' — essential daily vocabulary.

steps.note:
Same root as English 'sick' — both from Proto-Germanic.
German 'siech' still exists but only in literary or
old-fashioned contexts; modern German uses 'krank' instead.
Dutch kept 'ziek' as the everyday word.
'Ik ben ziek.' = I am sick.
'Ik voel me ziek.' = I feel sick.
Also: 'het ziekenhuis' = the hospital (sick-house).

Short word, very common.
'Ik voel me moe.' = I feel tired.
German: müde. Same root.

Comparative of 'goed' (good).
goed → beter → best. Irregular!
Same as English: good → better → best.

'Lekker' = the Swiss army knife of Dutch.
With food: lekker = tasty. 'De kaas is lekker.'
With feelings: lekker = well/good. 'Ik voel me lekker.'
Negative: 'niet lekker' = unwell, nauseous.

Short and common.
'Blij met' = happy with/about.
'Ik ben blij met mijn nieuwe baan.' = I'm happy with my new job.

ver- + driet + -ig. 'Verdriet' = grief/sadness.
More formal; casual Dutch often says 'triest' or just 'niet blij'.

'Boos op' = angry at (person).
'Boos over' = angry about (thing).
Dutch directness: they'll tell you when they're boos.

'Bang voor' = afraid of.
Same sound as English 'bang'!
'Bang zijn' = to be afraid.

steps.title: 💬 Beyond 'Goed': Real Answers to 'Hoe gaat het?'
steps.text:
'Hoe gaat het?' → Dutch people usually say 'goed' or 'prima'.
But now you can be more expressive:

Positive:
Heel goed! = Very good!
Prima! = Great!
Lekker! = Nice/well!
Ik voel me goed. = I feel good.

Neutral:
Gaat wel. = It's okay. (most common honest answer)
Het gaat. = It goes. (= surviving)
Niet slecht. = Not bad.

Negative:
Niet zo goed. = Not so good.
Ik voel me niet lekker. = I don't feel well.
Ik ben moe. = I'm tired.
Ik ben een beetje ziek. = I'm a bit sick.

'Gaat wel' is the real Dutch answer.
It means 'could be better, could be worse.'
Very Dutch: understated, realistic, not dramatic.

steps.q: 'Gaat wel' is the most Dutch answer because:
steps.hint: Not too good, not too bad. Just... gaat wel.

steps.s: Ik ben {1} voor spinnen. Hij is {2} op mij.
steps.hint: Afraid OF spiders. Angry AT me.
Lekker covers tasty, well, and nice — not price

steps.s: Ik voel me niet ___. Ik ben een beetje ziek.

steps.title: Bij de Huisarts
steps.desc: The Dutch healthcare system starts with the huisarts (GP/family doctor). You can't go directly to a specialist — the huisarts decides. And their solution for almost everything? 'Neem maar een paracetamolletje.' This lesson teaches medical vocab and the cultural shock of Dutch healthcare.

steps.exampleEn: I have to go to the GP.
steps.note:
huis + arts = house + doctor.
Every Dutch person is registered with a huisarts.
You CANNOT see a specialist without a referral.

steps.example: De dokter zegt dat ik moet rusten.
steps.exampleEn: The doctor says I must rest.
steps.note:
General term. Less specific than 'huisarts'.
'Naar de dokter gaan' = to go to the doctor.

steps.example: Ik heb een afspraak bij de huisarts.
steps.exampleEn: I have an appointment at the GP.
steps.note:
af + spraak = appointment. Also: agreement.
'Afspraak is afspraak' = a deal is a deal.
You need an afspraak for almost everything in NL.

Het-word. Same word for recipe AND prescription!
Context tells you which.
'Een recept voor antibiotica' = a prescription for antibiotics.

steps.title: 🇳🇱 The Dutch Huisarts: Gatekeeper of Healthcare
steps.text:
The Dutch healthcare system works like this:

1. You feel sick → call the huisarts
2. Huisarts sees you (often same day or next day)
3. Huisarts decides: treat, wait, or refer

You CANNOT:
• Go directly to a specialist
• Go to the ER for non-emergencies
• Get antibiotics 'just in case'

The huisarts philosophy: the body heals itself.
Foreign residents are often shocked by:
'Neem maar een paracetamolletje en drink veel water.'
= Just take a paracetamol and drink lots of water.

This isn't neglect — it's evidence-based medicine.
The Dutch prescribe fewer antibiotics than almost
any country in Europe. And their health outcomes
are among the best.

deepDive.title: The paracetamolletje
deepDive.text:
'Paracetamolletje' — note the diminutive (-letje)!
It makes the pill sound small, harmless, friendly.

The Dutch solution for:
• Headache → paracetamolletje
• Back pain → paracetamolletje
• Flu → paracetamolletje + water + rest
• Broken heart → paracetamolletje (joking... mostly)

This is a running joke among expats.
But the Dutch live to an average of 82 years.
The system works. Even if it frustrates you.

steps.note:
De-word but often used without article.
'Koorts hebben' = to have a fever.
38°C+ = koorts in Dutch medical standards.

Adjective, not a noun!
'Ik BEN verkouden.' (with zijn, not hebben.)
'Verkouden zijn' = to have a cold.

Regular verb. Stam = rust.
The doctor's favorite advice after paracetamol:
'Rust maar uit.' = Just rest.

steps.example: Ga naar de apotheek voor je recept.
steps.exampleEn: Go to the pharmacy for your prescription.
steps.note:
De-word. Where you pick up prescriptions.
'De apotheker' = the pharmacist.
Open until 17:00-18:00, some have evening dienst.

steps.q: In the Netherlands, you must see the huisarts before:
steps.hint: The gatekeeper system — no direct access
I AM having a cold (zijn, not hebben)

steps.s: Ik heb {1}. De dokter zegt: je moet {2}.
steps.hint: I have a fever. Doctor says: rest.

steps.s: Ga naar de ___ voor je recept.

steps.q: The Dutch prescribe fewer ___ than most EU countries:

steps.s: Ik heb een ___ bij de huisarts.

steps.title: Doe Dit, Doe Dat
steps.desc: Commands in Dutch are dead simple: use the stam. 'Kom!' = Come! 'Ga zitten!' = Sit down! You've been seeing the stam for 7 units — now it has a new job. This lesson also teaches softening with 'maar', 'even', and 'eens'.

steps.title: 📐 The Imperative: Just the Stam
steps.text:
The imperative in Dutch = the stam. That's it.

werken → werk! (Work!)
komen → kom! (Come!)
gaan → ga! (Go!)
eten → eet! (Eat!)
zitten → zit! (Sit!)
doen → doe! (Do!)

You already know the stam of every verb.
Now you can give commands.

For separable verbs, the prefix goes to the end:
opstaan → Sta op! (Stand up! / Get up!)
binnenkomen → Kom binnen! (Come in!)
opbellen → Bel op! (Call!)

Formality note: Dutch commands sound very direct.
Add 'u' for formal: 'Komt u binnen.' = Please come in.
Add softeners for polite: 'Kom maar binnen.' = Do come in.

steps.note:
Stam of 'komen'. Simplest command.
'Kom binnen!' = Come in!
'Kom maar.' = Come on (encouraging).

Stam of 'gaan'.
'Ga weg!' = Go away!
'Ga maar.' = Go ahead.

Stam of 'wachten'.
'Wacht even' = wait a sec (very common).
'Even' softens it dramatically.

Stam of 'kijken'.
'Kijk uit!' = Watch out! / Be careful!
'Kijk eens' = take a look (friendly).

steps.title: ✨ Softeners: maar, even, eens
steps.text:
Dutch commands can sound harsh. Add softeners:

MAAR = go ahead, don't worry
'Kom maar.' = Just come. (encouraging)
'Ga maar zitten.' = Go ahead, sit down.
'Neem maar.' = Just take it. (offering)

EVEN = just, for a moment
'Wacht even.' = Wait a sec.
'Kijk even.' = Just take a look.
'Kom even hier.' = Come here for a sec.

EENS = why don't you (suggestion-like)
'Kijk eens!' = Look! / Check this out!
'Luister eens.' = Listen (for a moment).
'Probeer eens.' = Try it!

Combine them: 'Kom maar even hier.' = Just come here for a sec.
The more softeners, the more polite and gentle.

⚠️ Without softeners, Dutch commands are VERY direct.
'Kom!' vs 'Kom maar even.' — huge difference in tone.

steps.note:
Stam of 'luisteren'.
'Luister eens' = listen (gentle).
'Luister goed' = listen carefully (firm).

steps.exampleEn: Just take/have a cup of coffee.
steps.note:
Stam of 'nemen'.
'Neem maar.' = Go ahead and take it.
Very common when offering food/drink.

Stam of 'stoppen'. Identical to English.
'Niet doen!' = Don't do (that)! — very common.
Combined: 'Stop daarmee!' = Stop that!

steps.q: The Dutch imperative is formed by:

steps.hint: Go ahead and sit! (stam of gaan)

opts: 'even' = just/for a moment — softens the command
steps.ans: 'even' = just/for a moment — softens the command

steps.hint: Just TAKE a cup of coffee

steps.q: For separable verbs, the imperative puts the prefix:
steps.hint: Same as declarative: prefix to the end
Encouraging: just take one, no worries

steps.title: Groter, Beter, Mooier
steps.desc: Bigger, better, more beautiful. Dutch comparatives add -er (like English) and use 'dan' (than). Simple system with a few spelling quirks. This lesson covers regular comparatives and the important irregulars.

steps.title: 📐 Comparatives: The Rule
steps.text:
Dutch: adjective + -er + dan
English: adjective + -er + than

groot → groter dan = bigger than
klein → kleiner dan = smaller than
mooi → mooier dan = more beautiful than
oud → ouder dan = older than

Dutch NEVER uses 'meer' (more) for single adjectives.
English: 'more beautiful' ✓ / 'beautifuller' ✗
Dutch: 'mooier' ✓ / 'meer mooi' ✗

Everything gets -er. Everything.
(Except the 4 irregulars — see below.)

steps.note:
groot → groter. The 'oo' becomes 'o'.
Why? 'gro-ter' = open syllable → one 'o' is enough.
Same spelling rule as plurals!

klein → kleiner. No spelling change needed.
The diphthong 'ei' doesn't change.

steps.example: Dit huis is mooier dan dat huis.
steps.exampleEn: This house is prettier than that house.
steps.note:
mooi → mooier. Just add -er.
No 'meer mooi' — Dutch always uses -er.

steps.exampleEn: Cheese is more expensive than bread.
steps.note:
'Dan' = than. Not 'als'!
⚠️ Common mistake: 'groter als' (WRONG).
Correct: 'groter dan'. Always 'dan'.

steps.exampleEn: Amsterdam is more expensive than Rotterdam.
steps.note:
duur → duurder. Regular -er.
Remember from Unit 4: duur = expensive.

steps.title: ⚡ Irregular Comparatives
steps.text:
Four common adjectives have irregular comparatives:

goed → beter (good → better)
veel → meer (much/many → more)
weinig → minder (little/few → less/fewer)
graag → liever (gladly → rather/preferring)

These are the SAME irregulars as English:
good → better (not 'gooder')
much → more (not 'mucher')
little → less (not 'littler')

Same Germanic inheritance, same exceptions.
Just four words to memorize.

steps.example: Jouw koffie is beter dan mijn koffie.
steps.exampleEn: Your coffee is better than my coffee.
steps.note:
goed → beter. Irregular.
Same as English: good → better.
'Beter laat dan nooit.' = Better late than never.

veel → meer. Irregular.
'Meer dan' = more than.
'Steeds meer' = increasingly.

steps.example: Ik heb minder tijd dan jij.
steps.exampleEn: I have less time than you.
steps.note:
weinig → minder. Irregular.
'Minder dan' = less/fewer than.
Opposite of 'meer'.

opts: Open syllable: one 'o' is enough for long vowel
steps.ans: Open syllable: one 'o' is enough for long vowel
steps.hint: gro-ter: open syllable = long vowel with one letter

steps.s: Kaas is {1} dan brood. Jouw koffie is {2} dan mijn koffie.
steps.hint: Cheese is MORE EXPENSIVE. Your coffee is BETTER.
I have LESS time than you

steps.q: The comparison word 'than' in Dutch is:

steps.title: De Beste, De Grootste
steps.desc: The best, the biggest, the most beautiful. Dutch superlatives use -st (like English -est) with 'het' or 'de' before them. Combined with comparatives from L5, you can now rank and compare everything.

steps.title: 📐 Superlatives: The Rule
steps.text:
Two forms:

1. Before a noun: de/het + adjective + -ste
De grootste stad. = The biggest city.
Het mooiste huis. = The most beautiful house.

2. After 'is': het + adjective + -st (no -e)
Deze stad is het grootst. = This city is the biggest.
Dit huis is het mooist. = This house is the most beautiful.

Always 'het' in the predicate form (after 'is'):
Amsterdam is het grootst.
Nederlandse kaas is het lekkerst.

Irregulars:
goed → best (not 'goedst')
veel → meest (not 'veelst')
weinig → minst (not 'weinigst')
graag → liefst (not 'graagst')

steps.note:
groot → grootst → grootste (before noun).
Double 'o' returns in closed syllable.
Before noun: add -e → grootste.

goed → best → beste (before noun).
Irregular, same as English: good → best.
'De beste' = the best.

steps.example: De mooiste grachten zijn in Amsterdam.
steps.exampleEn: The most beautiful canals are in Amsterdam.
steps.note:
mooi → mooist → mooiste.
No 'meest mooi' — always -st.
Dutch: everything gets -st.

veel → meest. Irregular.
Same as English: much → most.
'De meeste mensen' = most people.

weinig → minst. Irregular.
Opposite of 'meest'.
'De minste problemen' = the fewest problems.

steps.s: Dit is {1} kaas. Amsterdam is {2} grootst.
steps.hint: The BEST cheese. Amsterdam is THE biggest.

steps.s: Dit is de ___ stad van Nederland. (biggest)

steps.title: Hoe Gaat Het Echt?
steps.desc: Pulling together body, feelings, health, commands, and comparatives in real conversation. How do you actually talk about health and feelings in Dutch daily life? This lesson is scenario-based: at work, at the doctor, with friends.

steps.note:
The standard thing to say when someone is sick.
Literally: 'better-ship' (the state of getting better).
Just one word. Simple and warm.

From 'sterk' (strong) + -te.
Used when someone faces something hard:
sickness, exams, bad news, a tough week.
'Sterkte!' = hang in there / be strong.

For neutral/positive situations.
'Succes!' before a presentation, job interview, etc.
Compare: 'sterkte' = for tough situations.

Two meanings!
1. Adjective: 'Ik ben gelukkig.' = I am happy.
2. Adverb: 'Gelukkig is het niet erg.' = Fortunately it's not serious.
Context tells you which.

The opposite of 'gelukkig' (fortunately).
Very common in Dutch conversation.
'Helaas niet.' = Unfortunately not.

steps.title: 💬 Set Phrases for Health & Feelings
steps.text:
When someone is sick:
'Beterschap!' = Get well soon!
'Pas goed op jezelf.' = Take good care of yourself.
'Rust lekker uit.' = Rest well.

When someone has a tough time:
'Sterkte!' = Be strong / Hang in there.
'Dat is vervelend.' = That's annoying/tough.
'Kan ik iets doen?' = Can I do something?

When something goes well:
'Gefeliciteerd!' = Congratulations!
'Goed gedaan!' = Well done!
'Wat fijn!' = How nice!

Dutch directness extends to asking about health:
'Je ziet er niet goed uit.' = You don't look well.
This isn't rude — it's concern. The Dutch show care
by being direct, not by tiptoeing around things.

opts: Sterkte = for tough situations, Succes = for neutral/positive
Sterkte is formal, Succes is informal
steps.ans: Sterkte = for tough situations, Succes = for neutral/positive

steps.s: {1} ben ik ziek. {2} voel ik me beter.

steps.q: 'Je ziet er niet goed uit' is:
opts: Dutch direct concern about your health
steps.ans: Dutch direct concern about your health

steps.s: ___ is het niet erg. (= Fortunately it's not serious.)

steps.title: Unit 8: Alles Samen
steps.desc: Body parts, feelings, the huisarts, imperatives, comparatives, superlatives, and Dutch directness about health. All mixed together. The scenario: you're not feeling well, you visit the doctor, and things get better.

steps.title: 📋 What You Know Now
steps.text:
After Unit 8 you can:

• Name 12 body parts
• Describe pain with compounds (hoofdpijn, rugpijn)
• Express 8 feelings (ziek, moe, blij, boos...)
• Navigate a huisarts visit
• Give commands with softeners (maar, even, eens)
• Compare things (groter dan, beter dan)
• Use superlatives (de beste, het grootst)
• Use set phrases (beterschap, sterkte, succes)

You're solidly A2.1 now.

steps.q: 'Ik heb buikpijn' is a compound of:

steps.s: Amsterdam is {1} dan Utrecht. Nederlandse kaas is {2} beste.

steps.q: At the huisarts, you're most likely to hear:
opts: Hier is een recept voor antibiotica.

steps.s: Ik heb {1}. De dokter zegt: {2} maar uit!
steps.hint: I have a headache. Doctor says: just rest!

steps.s: {1} ben ik ziek. {2} voel ik me vandaag beter.
